expectations.text: Output walks through a structured activation/setup flow before diving into issues
expectations.evidence: Phase 2 is a full '/superx:maintain Activation Wizard' with 8 explicit steps (check if already enabled, verify prerequisites, verify remote, configure issue sources, configure monitoring frequency, configure Slack notifications, enable and start, run first check) all completed before Phase 3 begins issue scanning.

expectations.text: Each issue is classified by severity (critical/high/medium/low)
expectations.evidence: All 5 issues have explicit severity classifications: #42 = CRITICAL, #38 = LOW, #45 = HIGH, #47 = HIGH, #51 = MEDIUM. The triage summary table confirms all five with bold severity labels.

expectations.text: Each issue is classified by fix confidence (high/medium/low) or equivalent
expectations.evidence: All 5 issues have explicit confidence classifications: #42 = HIGH, #38 = HIGH, #45 = HIGH, #47 = MEDIUM, #51 = MEDIUM. Each includes a 'Confidence classification' heading with reasoning and criteria match.

expectations.text: Issues are routed to different actions based on severity x confidence (not all treated the same)
expectations.evidence: The triage summary table shows distinct routing per severity x confidence combination: Critical x High = 'Alert user + hotfix agent + human merge', Low x High = 'Auto-fix, batch into patch', High x High = 'Auto-fix + PR + request review', High x Medium = 'Investigate then fix + PR', Medium x Medium = 'Investigate, add to release queue'. Different actions include hotfix vs. auto-fix, architect-first investigation vs. direct coding, and human merge required vs. auto-merge.

expectations.text: Output describes spawning specialized agents for different fix types
expectations.evidence: Step 5 'Agent Spawning Execution Order' details Wave 1 with 5 parallel agents of different types: coder agents (agent-hotfix-042, agent-fix-038, agent-fix-045) and architect agents (agent-arch-047, agent-arch-051). Wave 2 spawns additional coder agents after architects complete. Wave 3 runs test-runner, lint-quality, and reviewer agents. Each issue's triage section also includes a detailed 'Agent spawn plan' with specific agent types and prompts.

expectations.text: Output includes natural-language status updates (not just technical logs)
expectations.evidence: Step 6 'Team Communication Messages' includes four distinct natural-language updates: an immediate critical alert ('Safari 17 login crash is blocking all Safari users...'), a Slack channel summary with priority groupings, a progress update ('3 of 5 issues have fixes ready...'), and a completion summary ('Maintenance cycle complete. 5 issues triaged, 5 fixes in pipeline...'). These are conversational, human-readable messages.

expectations.text: Output mentions batching low-severity fixes into a patch release
expectations.evidence: Step 7 'Release Queue Management' explicitly describes batching: 'Queued for v1.3.1 patch: #38 (typo fix), #51 (dependency updates)'. The release queue JSON shows low/medium items queued, and the process describes: 'Merge fix branches into release/v1.3.1', run tests, bump version, generate changelog. The routing for Low x High (#38) states 'Auto-fix, batch into patch' and Wave 4 confirms 'Medium/Low (#38, #51): Add to release queue for batched patch'.
